Thematic Review Series
Respiration 2004;71:209–213
DOI: 10.1159/000077416

# Pulmonary Function Testing in Interstitial Lung Diseases

Alfredo Chetta Emilio Marangio Dario Olivieri

Department of Clinical Sciences, Section of Respiratory Diseases, University of Parma, Parma, Italy

# Key Words

Interstitial lung disease W Pulmonary function testing W Exercise

# Abstract

Interstitial lung diseases (ILDs) are functionally characterized by a restrictive ventilatory defect due to a reduced distensibility of the lung parenchyma. ILD patients also show a reduced exercise tolerance, the main factors limiting exercise capacity being ventilatory and gas exchange abnormalities. Functional abnormalities in ILDs are typical, but not specific. Despite the fact that different lung function patterns have been described among ILDs, they overlap and their practical application to differentiate ILDs is poor. Resting pulmonary function and exercise-induced hypoxemia can aid in defining the prognosis of ILDs and in referring patients for lung transplantation. Additionally, spirometry and diffusing capacity are useful to monitor the response of patients to therapy. This review specifically addresses the role of pulmonary function testing in ILDs.

# Functional Abnormalities in ILD

In theory, several mechanisms, such as loss of lung volume, reduction in alveolar distensibility and/or in alveolar size and increased surface tension due to surfactant abnormalities, can affect lung mechanics in ILDs. Consequently, ILDs are associated with contraction of the static pressure-volume curve, thereby reducing lung volumes and lung compliance and increasing lung recoil pressure at a given absolute lung volume.

pages.md:
The reduced distensibility of lung parenchyma determines small tidal volumes, and an adequate ventilation can be maintained at the cost of a rapid breathing frequency [8]. Moreover, the breathing pattern may change with disease severity; lower lung volumes being associated with an increased respiratory rate and decreased tidal volume [8].

In patients with ILDs, static lung volumes are not uniformly reduced. The change in vital capacity (VC) is usually greater than that of functional residual capacity (FRC) and that of residual volume (RV) [7]. Consequently, the total lung capacity (TLC) is usually less reduced than VC and the RV/TLC ratio may be greater than normal in ILDs. The mechanism by which FRC and RV may be preserved is not clear; however, it may be a consequence of the premature closure of small airways and air trapping or of cystic changes in lung parenchyma. Although the small airways may be narrowed by fibrosis or inflammatory infiltrates, the patency of large airways is usually preserved in uncomplicated ILDs and volume-corrected indices of airflow, such as the ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 s to VC (FEV1/VC) is normal or greater than normal [8].

Diffusing capacity (DLCO) is typically reduced in ILD and represents the most sensitive simple test of respiratory function [9]. Reduced lung volumes and poor distribution of inspired gas can contribute to the reduction of DLCO in ILD patients. DLCO is reduced to a greater extent than the lung volume at which it is measured, so that the carbon monoxide transfer coefficient (KCO) is also reduced [9]. In ambulatory patients with ILDs, a resting arterial hypoxemia may be present, while arterial hypercapnia seems to be rare [8]. An oxygen desaturation can be detected during sleep and the change in oxygen saturation is greater in patients whose awake values are lower [10]. Both ventilation/perfusion mismatching and alveolar-capillary block are currently considered as the two relevant determining factors of the abnormal gas exchange in ILDs [11].

Despite the assorted causes of ILDs, the responses to exercise demonstrated by these diseases are generally similar (table 1, fig. 2). Ventilatory and gas exchange abnormalities are considered the main factors which limit exercise capacity in ILDs, though cardiac dysfunction, unfitness and dyspnea may also contribute [12]. Gas exchange abnormality, with hypoxemia prevailing, is extremely evident during exercise. Ventilation-perfusion mismatch and shunt are considered to be responsible for most of the increase in the alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient during exercise [11, 13]. Limited diffusion [11, 13] and low mixed venous arterial oxygen tension [13] can significantly contribute to this increased gradient. Oxygen desaturation during exercise can be highly predicted by DLCO [14, 15], even if it shows only a modest correlation with static lung volumes [15].

# Pulmonary Function Tests in Differential Diagnosis

In sarcoidosis patients with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, lung function is often normal [16]. In contrast, sarcoidosis patients with diffuse interstitial infiltrates usually present a mild restrictive defect [16]. Furthermore, patients with pulmonary sarcoidosis frequently demonstrate less impaired lung function than those with other ILDs, such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (HPF). Despite similar lung volumes, when patients with HPF were compared with those with sarcoidosis, they had lower DLCO values [17]. Among patients with HPF, discordant results were found when patients with nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) were compared with those with usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP), which are two forms of HPF with different survival rates. One report found higher values of TLC, but not of forced vital capacity (FVC) and of DLCO in patients with NSIP, as compared to those with UIP [18]. On the other hand, another report found no difference in FVC, but higher values of DLCO in NSIP patients with respect to UIP patients [19]. A similar degree of hypoxemia was reported between UIP and NSIP patients in two studies [18, 20].

Fig. 1. Incremental exercise performance in a male patient aged 56 years with ILD, presenting the basic plots recommended for the interpretation of cardiopulmonary exercise test. The patient showed a low peak of O2 uptake, which was 1,308 ml (54% of predicted) (a). The anaerobic threshold was detected at 1,097 ml of O2 uptake (46% of predicted peak of O2 uptake), which was at the lower limit of the normal range (a–d). The marked limitation of exercise tolerance was confirmed by the low peak of work rate, which was 103 W (64% of predicted) (e). b, f, h show the patient’s ventilatory limitation. Minute ventilation at peak exercise was low (61 liters/min) and similar to the maximal attainable ventilation (63 liters/min), such that the breathing reserve was only 3% (f). The tidal volume (h) showed a plateau at a low level of minute ventilation (F30 liters/min). The ventilatory equivalent of O2 and CO₂ were high at the anaerobic threshold (b). Moreover, by pulsoxymetry the O2 saturation was 96% at rest and 85% at peak exercise. Finally, the cardiac function showed a normal response to exercise. The slope of heart rate versus the O2 uptake was in the normal range (g).

pages.md:
study found a lower arterial oxygen tension in NSIP patients as compared to that of UIP patients [19]. Moreover, both patients above and patients below 65% of predicted value of VC and DLCO showed similar patterns of spontaneous variations in disease [16]. However, it has been shown that an initial VC of less than 80% of predicted can independently influence the persistence of disease activity over time [32]. Moreover, reduced DLCO was associated to sarcoidosis patients who worsened, while normal or near normal DLCO was associated with patients with stable disease [33].

In systemic diseases with pulmonary parenchyma involvement, pulmonary function tests can provide some information on the disease course. In patients with rheumatoid arthritis and ILD, increased mortality was significantly even if modestly correlated to low VC, but not to low DLCO [34]. In systemic sclerosis with pulmonary involvement, a high prognostic value of DLCO to discriminate patients with different survival rates was also reported. Among systemic sclerosis patients with DLCO less than 40% of predicted, 5-year survival was only 9%, while it reached 75% when the DLCO was greater than 40% of predicted [35].

In monitoring the response to therapy, VC and DLCO are the most used parameters in ILDs. A change in VC greater than 10–15% and greater than 20% in DLCO was considered as significant both in IPF [28, 36, 37] and sarcoidosis patients [38, 39]. In IPF patients, when pulmonary function becomes abnormal, even though the patient may be minimally symptomatic, serious consideration should be given to referral to a transplantation center for initial evaluation. Besides a symptomatic disease unresponsive to medical therapy, a VC less than 60–70% of predicted and the evidence of resting- or exercise-induced hypoxemia are considered as selection criteria for referral for lung transplantation [40].

# Conclusions

In summary, functional abnormalities in ILDs are typical, but not specific. Despite the fact that different lung function patterns have been described among ILDs, they overlap and their practical application to differentiate ILDs is poor. Resting pulmonary function and exercise-induced hypoxemia can aid to define the prognosis of ILDs and for referral for lung transplantation. Additionally, VC and DLCO are useful parameters to monitor the response of patients to therapy.
